quiz.prompt: A client's PoC churn model hits 0.88 AUC in a notebook. The sponsor asks: "How long to deploy it — and then we're done, right?" What's the honest answer?

choices.text: Deployment starts a loop, not finishes a project: the model is ~5-10% of the system, and the recurring part — monitoring, retraining, on-call, run cost — runs until decommissioning. The scoping question is "who runs this in month seven?"
choices.explanation: This is the module's core reframe: shipping a model is taking on a liability, not banking an asset. The training project is a one-off cost; the loop (serve → monitor → retrain) is recurring. Most failed AI programmes priced the launch and forgot the loop.

choices.text: About a week — wrap the notebook in an API, copy it to a server, and hand it to IT.
choices.explanation: "Just copy the notebook to the server" is a named red flag: no container, no reproducibility, no validation gates, no monitoring. It produces a deployment you can only hope about — and nobody owns it when it degrades.

choices.text: The model is done; the remaining work is purely an IT integration task with no ML-specific content.
choices.explanation: The system around the model — data validation, serving, monitoring, drift detection, retraining, registry — is precisely the ML-specific work, and it's most of the effort. Treating it as generic IT is why most AI initiatives quietly die in the notebook→product gap.

choices.text: Quite a while — the main remaining work is pushing the AUC higher before production.
choices.explanation: Squeezing more offline AUC doesn't close the production gap at all. A 0.88 model that runs reliably, monitored, on live data beats a 0.91 model in a notebook — and the live AUC will drift regardless, which is what the loop exists to catch.

quiz.takeaway: Production ML is a loop, not a launch: the model is the small box, and the question that completes the scoping is "who runs this in month seven?"

quiz.prompt: A CRM team will act on a list of at-risk customers each morning. The engineering team proposes a real-time prediction API "so the architecture is future-proof." What should you decide?

choices.text: Batch — a nightly scoring job serves a morning-consumed list perfectly, and the real-time API would add an SLA, an on-call rota, and a multiple of the run cost for zero added value.
choices.explanation: Default to batch; escalate only when a human or system genuinely cannot wait. The consumption pattern (a list, read each morning) sets the requirement, not architectural ambition. "Future-proof" here means "permanently more expensive."

choices.text: The real-time API — fresher predictions are always more valuable, and the team will eventually need it anyway.
choices.explanation: Fresher than the decision cadence is waste: the list is consumed once a day, so sub-second scores change nothing. "Real-time" is a cost decision dressed as a technical one — always-on means someone is accountable at 2am, and that's staffing, not just infra.

choices.text: Streaming — churn signals arrive continuously, so the pipeline should process events as they happen.
choices.explanation: Streaming has the highest plumbing cost (platform + state + specialist skills) and fits decisions made on a continuous flow. The decision here happens once a day, in a morning queue. Events arriving continuously doesn't mean decisions are made continuously.

choices.text: Split the difference: real-time API, but with a relaxed SLA to keep costs down.
choices.explanation: An always-on service is an always-on service — autoscaling, deployment surface, failure modes, and someone watching it. A "relaxed SLA" doesn't remove the standing infrastructure or the operational ownership; batch removes both.

quiz.takeaway: Default to batch and escalate only when someone genuinely cannot wait — a real-time API for a morning-consumed list buys an SLA and an on-call rota for nothing.

quiz.prompt: Six months after launch, a fraud model's dashboard shows 100% uptime and healthy latency, but the business reports rising fraud losses. What's the most likely explanation?

choices.text: The model is rotting silently — fraud tactics shifted (concept drift), and the monitoring watches only operational health, so nothing alerted; a model can be 100% "up" and increasingly wrong.
choices.explanation: Software fails loudly; models fail silently — confident, well-formed, increasingly wrong answers with no stack trace. Fraud is a fast-drifting domain (adversaries adapt). Monitoring only latency/uptime is the named trap: easy technical metrics standing in for the hard one that matters.

choices.text: If uptime and latency are healthy, the model is fine — the losses must come from a business-process problem.
choices.explanation: This is exactly the trap. Operational health says the service answers; it says nothing about whether the answers are right. The live business metric is the verdict, and it's pointing at the model.

choices.text: This must be data drift — the input features have visibly changed distribution.
choices.explanation: Not necessarily — with adaptive fraud, the input→output relationship can shift while inputs look familiar; that's concept drift, and input-distribution monitors can stay quiet through it. The distinction matters because the detection signals differ (input drift vs falling accuracy once labels arrive).

choices.text: Retrain immediately on the latest data — diagnosis can wait until after the metric recovers.
choices.explanation: Retraining blind is a gamble: if a pipeline broke or labels shifted, you retrain on the problem. First confirm what moved (inputs, predictions, outcome metric), then retrain through a validation gate so a worse model can't ship in a panic.

quiz.takeaway: Models fail silently: monitor the outcome metric, model quality, and drift — not just uptime and latency — because a model can be 100% up and 100% wrong.

quiz.prompt: A monitoring dashboard shows PSI = 0.31 on two key features of a churn model. Ground truth (actual churn) won't arrive for weeks. How do you read this, and what do you do?

choices.text: PSI > 0.25 signals significant drift — the model is seeing a different world than it trained on. Treat it as an early warning: investigate now (and likely retrain), rather than waiting weeks for the outcome metric to confirm the damage.
choices.explanation: That's the standard threshold reading: < 0.1 stable, 0.1–0.25 watch, > 0.25 investigate and likely retrain. Because ground truth is late, input drift is the early-warning system and the lagging outcome metric is the verdict — waiting for the verdict means weeks of degraded decisions.

choices.text: PSI of 0.31 is below the alarm threshold of 0.5, so no action is needed yet.
choices.explanation: There is no 0.5 threshold in the standard rule of thumb — 0.25 is already the "significant drift" line. Misremembering the thresholds turns the early-warning system into a late-warning system.

choices.text: PSI proves accuracy has dropped, so ship a retrained model today without further checks.
choices.explanation: PSI measures input distribution shift, not accuracy — it's a leading indicator, not the verdict. And any retrained candidate still has to clear the model-validation gate (metric floor, no regression); drift is never a licence to skip validation.

choices.text: Ignore PSI until the actual churn labels arrive — only the real outcome metric justifies action.
choices.explanation: That defeats the entire purpose of drift monitoring. Ground truth is late by nature here; input drift exists precisely so you can act before the lagging metric confirms what the drift already told you.

quiz.takeaway: PSI > 0.25 means the model is seeing a different world than it trained on — investigate and likely retrain now, because waiting for late ground truth means weeks of silent damage.

quiz.prompt: For a newly productionised document-classification model in a stable domain, the client asks: "Should we set up automated nightly retraining from day one, to be safe?" What's the right call?

choices.text: No — monitor first, retrain manually on alert, and automate the cadence only once you've observed how fast this model actually decays. Automated retraining is a capability you earn, not one you ship on day one.
choices.explanation: The cadence should be set by how fast the domain moves — fraud drifts in days, a document classifier may be stable for a year. Each retrain costs compute, engineer time, and a fresh validation + re-approval; nightly retraining a stable model burns money and risks shipping a worse model from a noisy day.

choices.text: Yes — more frequent retraining is always safer, since the model can only get more current.
choices.explanation: Retraining isn't free and isn't always better: a noisy week can produce a worse model, and every cycle requires validation and re-approval before shipping. "Always fresher" is a calendar habit, not a cost-benefit argument.

choices.text: Yes, but skip the validation step on automated retrains to keep the pipeline fast.
choices.explanation: You cannot ship an unvalidated model — that's the hard rule. An automated pipeline without a validation gate is a machine for occasionally deploying regressions at high speed, with nobody watching.

choices.text: No — retrain only when the client complains that predictions seem off.
choices.explanation: That replaces monitoring with the client as your alerting system — the most expensive drift detector there is. The trigger should come from drift/decay monitoring crossing agreed thresholds, not from eroded trust.

quiz.takeaway: Tie retraining cadence to observed decay, not to a calendar habit: monitor first, retrain manually on alert, and automate only once you know how fast this model actually rots.

quiz.prompt: A client says: "We already have solid CI/CD — every deploy runs the full test suite, all green. So our ML deployment pipeline is covered." What's missing?

choices.text: The two ML-specific gates: data validation (schema, ranges, null rates on incoming data) and model validation (metric above a floor on held-out data, no regression vs the production model) — because an ML system can break via bad data or a bad model while every code test stays green.
choices.explanation: In ML the artefact is code + data + a trained model, and software tests assert only the first. A green suite tells you the code runs; it says nothing about whether the data is sane or the model is any good. That's why "we have CI" ≠ "we have ML CI."

choices.text: Nothing significant — a trained model is just another build artefact, so standard CI/CD covers it.
choices.explanation: A model passes every unit test while being silently terrible: trained on broken upstream data, or simply worse than the version it replaces. Standard CI/CD has no gate that would catch either failure — they're invisible to code tests by construction.

choices.text: Canary deployments — gradual traffic shifting is the ML-specific addition to CI/CD.
choices.explanation: Canary/A-B rollout is valuable (it's a maturity-level-3 capability), but it's a release technique, not the missing gate. Without data and model validation, a canary just exposes a smaller share of users to an unvalidated model first.

choices.text: Faster pipelines — ML models are big, so the gap is mainly build performance and artifact storage.
choices.explanation: Build speed and artifact handling are engineering conveniences. The structural gap is that ML breaks in two ways software can't — bad data and bad models — and both sail through a software-only pipeline unchecked.

quiz.takeaway: ML CI/CD is software CI/CD plus two gates — data validation and model validation — because bad data and bad models both pass every code test.

quiz.prompt: Your team productionised classic ML models before; now a GenAI assistant built on a hosted LLM API is going live. What's the most important shift in operational focus?

choices.text: You don't own or retrain the model, so the weight shifts from training pipelines to prompts-as-versioned-deploys, behavioural eval suites, per-feature token-cost and latency monitoring, guardrails, and end-to-end tracing.
choices.explanation: That's the LLMOps wrinkle: a prompt edit is a deploy, the vendor can update the model under you (silent drift you don't control), cost moves with traffic and prompt length, and multi-step apps are undebuggable without tracing. Classic MLOps front-loaded retraining; LLMOps front-loads evals, guardrails, tracing, and a cost dashboard.

choices.text: Operations get simpler — the vendor runs the model, so monitoring and versioning are mostly their problem now.
choices.explanation: The vendor runs the weights; everything that makes your feature behave — prompts, retrieved context, guardrails, spend — is yours. Worse, an unannounced vendor model update is drift you didn't cause and can't prevent, which makes your own eval suite more necessary, not less.

choices.text: The core new work is setting up pipelines to retrain the LLM regularly on company data.
choices.explanation: You typically rent the model via an API and don't retrain it at all — that's precisely why the operational weight moves elsewhere. Importing classic retraining reflexes into an LLM app means investing in the one lever you don't have.

choices.text: Pick a single accuracy metric for the assistant and wire it into the existing model-validation gate, as with any model.
choices.explanation: There's rarely a single accuracy number for open-ended generation. You assemble a graded behavioural test suite — often LLM-as-judge plus human spot-checks — covering quality, safety, and regression. The validation spirit carries over; the single-metric mechanics don't.

quiz.takeaway: LLMOps shifts the operational weight from training pipelines to prompts, evals, token cost, guardrails, and tracing — because the model is rented and can change under you.

quiz.prompt: A client at MLOps maturity level 0 (notebooks, manual deploys, no monitoring) asks you to commit to a 99.99%-uptime scoring API. How should you respond?

choices.text: Reframe it: four nines allows ~52 minutes of downtime a year and is a staffing-and-failover commitment, with each extra nine costing roughly 10× the engineering — so first ask what uptime the use case truly needs, and scope the honest next maturity rung instead.
choices.explanation: An SLA is a staffing decision: if nobody owns nights, you have a hope, not an SLA. Most clients asking for "AI in production" are at level 0 imagining level 3 — your value is naming the real rung and pricing the run cost they didn't see, not selling reliability they can't operate.

choices.text: Accept — modern cloud platforms make four nines essentially automatic.
choices.explanation: The platform's own SLA doesn't cover your model service, your data dependencies, or your deploys — and 52 minutes/year of allowed downtime demands failover and an on-call rota. Promising an SLA you haven't staffed is the fastest route to a furious client.

choices.text: Counter with 99% — it sounds almost the same and is much easier to hit.
choices.explanation: Two nines permits ~3.65 days of downtime a year — a wildly different promise than four nines' ~52 minutes, and "sounds similar" is exactly how SLA conversations go wrong. The right move is deriving the requirement from the use case, not haggling over nines.

choices.text: Accept, but plan to absorb any breaches with contract penalty clauses rather than engineering.
choices.explanation: Penalties price the failure; they don't prevent it, and a repeatedly-down model service destroys the client relationship the penalties were meant to protect. The error-budget framing is the honest tool: an SLA grants a spendable failure allowance you manage with engineering and staffing.

quiz.takeaway: An SLA is a staffing decision — each extra nine costs ~10× the engineering — so name the client's real maturity rung and sell the next one, not a reliability level nobody can operate.

exercises.title: Design the drift-monitoring and retraining plan
exercises.scenario:
A logistics client runs a delivery-time prediction model you productionised three months ago: nightly batch scoring, predictions consumed by the dispatch team each morning. Ground truth (actual delivery time) arrives within ~2 days. This morning the monitoring dashboard shows: PSI on parcel_weight = 0.08, PSI on origin_depot = 0.18, PSI on destination_zone = 0.31. The live error metric (MAE) is still within its agreed floor. The client's ops lead asks: "One feature is flashing red — do we retrain tonight?"

They also want, in writing, the standing monitoring-and-retraining plan you should have given them at handover.
givens: Serving: nightly batch; dispatch team acts on the scores each morning
Ground-truth lag: ~2 days (fast, by ML standards)
Agreed quality floor: live MAE may degrade at most 15% vs the validation baseline before action is mandatory
PSI rule-of-thumb thresholds: < 0.1 stable; 0.1–0.25 moderate (watch); > 0.25 significant drift
Context the client mentions in passing: a new e-commerce customer was onboarded two weeks ago, shipping to regions the company rarely served before
tasks: Interpret the three PSI readings and answer the "retrain tonight?" question with a justified yes/no and the action you take instead (or as well).
Write the standing monitoring plan: what is monitored, in priority order, with thresholds and who gets alerted.
Define the retraining strategy: trigger(s), validation gating, and why you would or wouldn't automate the cadence at this stage.
State what you commit to the client and what you explicitly do not promise.
exercises.solution:
Step 1 — read the dashboard, answer the question. parcel_weight (0.08) is stable; origin_depot (0.18) is a moderate shift — watch it; destination_zone (0.31) is past the 0.25 line: significant drift — the model is seeing destinations it didn't train on. The likely cause is sitting in the givens: the new e-commerce customer shipping to rarely-served regions. So: no, don't retrain tonight on reflex — investigate today. Two reasons: (1) the live MAE is still within its floor, and with only ~2 days of ground-truth lag you'll see real degradation almost immediately if it comes; (2) drift here is explained and expected (a known business event — this is the event-driven trigger firing, not a mystery). The right same-day actions: slice live MAE by destination zone — if errors concentrate in the new regions, schedule a retrain with the new customer's data included as soon as enough labelled deliveries exist; meanwhile, flag to dispatch that predictions for the new zones carry lower confidence. Retraining "tonight" on two weeks of thin data for the new zones could easily ship a worse model — and any candidate must clear validation anyway.

Step 2 — the standing monitoring plan (priority order).

  1. Business/outcome metric: on-time-dispatch performance attributable to the model's queue — the verdict (weekly review with ops).
  2. Model quality: live MAE vs the validation baseline, computed as ground truth lands (~2-day lag); alert at 10% degradation (warning), 15% (action floor — pages the owning engineer).
  3. Input drift: PSI per key feature vs the training baseline, nightly; warn at 0.1–0.25, alert at > 0.25 — the early-warning layer for the day ground truth ever slows down.
  4. Prediction drift: distribution of predicted delivery times, nightly — catches silent pipeline breakage even before labels.
  5. Operational health: did the nightly job run, on time, with expected row counts (freshness + volume). A scoring job that silently skipped a night is confidently-stale data by morning. Every alert names a human: the runbook lists the owning engineer and the escalation path.

Step 3 — retraining strategy. Triggers, in order of trust: (a) decay-triggered — live MAE crosses the 15% floor → mandatory retrain; (b) drift-triggered — PSI > 0.25 sustained a week on a key feature → investigate, retrain if error slices confirm; (c) event-driven — the business tells us the world changed (new customer, new regions — exactly today's case) → plan a retrain as labelled data accumulates. Every candidate passes the validation gate: beats the floor on a held-out recent window, no regression vs production, registered with lineage before deploy. Do not automate the cadence yet: three months in, we haven't observed a full decay cycle. Monitor first, retrain manually on alert, automate only once the decay rate is known — automated retraining is earned, not shipped on day one.

Step 4 — commitments. Committed: a monitored loop — thresholds, alerts that page a named owner, a documented retraining trigger and runbook, and a response within an agreed time when the floor is breached. Explicitly not promised: a fixed accuracy forever (you commit to a floor and a response, because the world drifts), retraining on any fixed calendar regardless of signal, or "set and forget."

keyPoints: PSI readings interpreted correctly against the 0.1 / 0.25 thresholds, with 0.31 identified as significant drift linked to the known business event
"Retrain tonight?" answered as investigate-first — justified by the stable MAE, the fast ground-truth lag, and the risk of retraining on thin new-zone data
Monitoring plan in priority order: outcome metric and model quality above drift, drift above operational health, with thresholds and a named alerted human
Retraining defined with explicit triggers and a validation gate, and automation deliberately deferred until the decay rate has been observed
pitfalls: Reflex-retraining on a drift alert without diagnosis — risking a worse model trained on two weeks of thin data, and skipping the validation gate under time pressure
Monitoring only operational health (job ran, latency fine) — the named trap that lets a model be 100% up and increasingly wrong
Ignoring the event-driven signal: the business told you the world changed; drift monitoring just confirmed it
Promising a fixed accuracy or fully-automated retraining on a three-month-old deployment
takeaways: Drift metrics are the early warning and the outcome metric is the verdict — when ground truth is fast, use it; when it's slow, drift is all you have
Retraining is a triggered, validated, owned process — not a calendar habit and not a panic response
rubric.transfer: This is the universal pattern for operating any decision system whose environment moves: leading indicators with thresholds, a lagging verdict metric, diagnosis before intervention, and changes gated by validation. The same loop runs credit-risk scorecard governance in banks, A/B-tested pricing engines, even infrastructure capacity planning — and the "investigate before you retrain" discipline is the ops sibling of "diagnose before you medicate" in incident response: acting on an alert without understanding it converts one incident into two.

exercises.title: Price the loop, not the launch
exercises.scenario:
An insurer has a claims-fraud PoC (notebook, ~0.86 AUC) and wants it "live for the fraud team." The fraud analysts work a prioritised queue each morning; claims arrive continuously but nothing is auto-blocked — a flagged claim just gets reviewed sooner. An eager solution architect has already sketched a real-time scoring API on an autoscaling cluster with a 99.9% SLA.

The client asks you to validate the architecture and produce the budget: build cost and — for the first time, they admit — the recurring run cost. The client has never run a model in production before (no registry, no monitoring, manual everything).
givens: Consumption pattern: fraud analysts triage a queue each morning; no automated blocking, no system needs sub-second answers
Real-time option: always-on serving ≈ 1,100 euros/month infra, plus a 99.9% SLA implying staffed on-call (three nines = ~8.8 hours allowed downtime/year)
Batch option: nightly scoring job ≈ 90 euros/month compute; no SLA beyond "scores ready by 7am", no on-call rota
Either way: monitoring + alerting tooling ≈ 150 euros/month; estimated 1–3 engineer-days/month to watch drift, investigate alerts, and retrain on trigger (fully loaded ≈ 600 euros/day)
Agreed quality floor: live AUC ≥ 0.81 (vs 0.86 at validation), with a defined response when breached
tasks: Choose the serving pattern and justify it against the consumption pattern; state what the real-time proposal would have cost the client in money and obligations.
Itemise the build (one-off) and the run (recurring) — then put a monthly number on the run cost for your recommended design.
Place the client on the MLOps maturity ladder and state the honest promise you can make at the next rung.
Write the three-sentence budget summary for the sponsor, including what you do not promise.
exercises.solution:
Step 1 — serving pattern. The fraud team acts on a morning queue: predictions can be precomputed. That's the batch signature. Nightly batch scoring, writing scores into the queue before 7am. The real-time API would have cost ~1,100 euros/month in standing infra versus ~90 — but the bigger items are the obligations: a 99.9% SLA means ~8.8 hours/year of allowed downtime, which means failover and a staffed on-call rota for a client that has never operated a model. A real-time API for a morning-consumed queue is the textbook self-inflicted SLA. (If the client later automates blocking decisions at claim-submission time, that's a genuinely different use case — re-scope then; don't pre-build for it now.)

Step 2 — build vs run. Build (one-off, ~4–6 weeks): containerise the model (no loose .pkl); scheduled nightly scoring job writing to the queue; registry entry with data/code lineage; data-validation gate on the input feed (schema, ranges, nulls); drift (PSI) + live-AUC monitoring with thresholds; runbook naming the owner and the retraining trigger. The model itself is the smallest part of this list — say so. Run (recurring, monthly): compute ~90; monitoring tooling ~150; people: 1–3 engineer-days/month at ~600 euros/day = 600–1,800. Total ≈ 840–2,040 euros/month, headline ≈ 1,500 euros/month — versus ~3,000+ for the real-time design before counting on-call staffing. The people line dominates, which is the point clients miss: the run cost is mostly humans watching the loop, and it never stops.

Step 3 — maturity ladder. The client is at level 0 (manual everything). This engagement delivers level 1 — Repeatable: containerised serving, a registry, basic drift monitoring. The honest promise at that rung: "reproducible deploys, and we'll see decay before your fraud losses do." Do not promise level-2 automated retraining on a green-field engagement — it's earned after the loop has run manually and the decay rate is known.

Step 4 — sponsor summary. "We recommend nightly batch scoring: it serves your team's morning queue identically to a real-time API at roughly half the monthly cost and without an on-call rota you'd have to staff. Build is a fixed ~4–6 week engagement; the run cost is ~1,500 euros/month ongoing — mostly the engineering time that watches drift and retrains when the live AUC slips below the agreed 0.81 floor. We commit to a monitored loop with a named owner, an alert threshold, and a defined response — not to a fixed accuracy forever, an unstaffed SLA, or set-and-forget."

keyPoints: Batch chosen from the consumption pattern (morning queue), with the real-time option costed and rejected for both money and obligations (SLA = staffing)
Build and run itemised separately, with a concrete monthly run figure and recognition that the people line dominates the run cost
Client placed at maturity level 0 with the engagement scoped to level 1 and the matching honest promise
Commitments framed as floor + response (AUC ≥ 0.81, alert, retrain), never a fixed accuracy or an unstaffed SLA
pitfalls: Validating the real-time architecture because it's already sketched — inheriting an SLA, an on-call rota, and a cost multiple for zero decision value
Quoting only the build and leaving the recurring run cost (especially the engineer-days) off the budget — the module's central failure
Selling automated retraining or a high maturity rung to a level-0 org that can't operate it after you leave
Promising an accuracy number forever instead of a monitored floor with a defined response
takeaways: The sellable, honest deliverable is the loop with its run cost made explicit — a monitored, owned, retrainable system priced monthly, not a one-time deployment
Match the serving pattern to the decision cadence and the operational machinery to the client's real maturity rung
rubric.transfer: Pricing the loop instead of the launch is the TCO discipline that applies to anything with a recurring operational tail: infrastructure (the cluster is cheap, the on-call isn't), security tooling (the SIEM licence vs the analysts who triage it), even content platforms (the CMS vs the editorial workflow). Whenever a buyer prices the visible artefact and forgets the humans who keep it true, the consultant's job is to surface the recurring line before the contract is signed — it protects the client's budget and your margin simultaneously.
